subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that a clock is used to tell time.
Your child can tell what hour it is on a digital clock.
Your child can tell and write time to the nearest minute, measure time intervals, and solve problems adding or subtracting time.
Your child can read the hour on a digital clock.
Your child can tell the hour on a digital clock.
Your child can solve problems adding or subtracting time in minutes using pictures or tools, like finding the total time of two events.
Your child can tell time to the nearest five minutes on both analog and digital clocks and use a.m. and p.m. correctly.
Your child can solve one- and two-step problems about elapsed time.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Time — Reading Clocks and Solving Elapsed Time Problems
coherence_reason: All standards focus on telling time and solving problems involving elapsed time, specifically with digital and analog clocks.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can identify the costs and benefits of planned and unplanned spending.
Your child can explain how having more or fewer resources affects prices and costs.
Your child can figure out the total value of a collection of coins and bills.
Your child can recognize choices that involve earning money, spending, saving, borrowing, and giving to charity.
Your child understands that credit is used when you need more money than you have and that it’s important to pay back what you owe, usually with extra fees.
Your child can make a list of reasons to save money and explain how a savings plan helps, including saving for college.
Your child understands how people’s skills and work relate to the money they earn.
Your child can make change up to $5.00 using coins and bills, giving no more than $2.00 back.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Financial Literacy — Money, Earning, and Spending
coherence_reason: The standards cover related financial literacy concepts but include two distinct sub-concepts: understanding and calculating money, and broader economic decision-making (spending, saving, earning, credit).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child learns about area and how it relates to multiplying and adding.
Your child knows a square with sides one unit long is called a unit square and has an area of one square unit to measure area.
Your child can connect finding area with multiplying and adding numbers.
Your child can solve problems about the perimeter of shapes, including finding missing side lengths and comparing shapes with the same perimeter or area.
Your child can find the area of shapes by counting squares of different sizes like square centimeters or square inches.
Your child understands that perimeter measures the distance around a shape and can tell the difference between length and area.
Your child understands that area is a way to describe flat shapes and learns how to measure it.
Your child can multiply whole number side lengths to find the area of rectangles and use this to solve problems.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Measurement — Area and Perimeter
coherence_reason: The standards cover both area and perimeter, which are related measurement concepts but distinct; some focus exclusively on area, others on perimeter.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve simple real-life problems by counting up to 5 objects.
Your child can tell which group has more or fewer objects.
Your child can recognize numbers like 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 when shown with objects or models.
Your child uses numbers like 10, 20, and 30 to help understand and work with numbers from 0 to 30.
Your child can use repeated addition (adding the same number over and over) to find a total up to 20.
Your child can count by tens up to 50 using blocks or coins.
Your child can count by tens up to 100 using objects, blocks, or money.
Your child can count by tens using things like objects, blocks, or coins.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Numbers and Operations — Counting, Comparing, and Grouping by Tens
coherence_reason: The standards address related but distinct sub-concepts: counting and comparing small quantities, recognizing numbers with models, and repeated addition/grouping by tens.
split_topics: Counting and Grouping by Tens/Repeated Addition

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.
Model with mathematics.
Your child uses the right kind of language when talking in both casual and formal situations.
Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.
Your child uses strategies to read smoothly and understand different kinds of stories and books.
Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.
Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.
Your child can use different strategies to read and understand informational texts smoothly.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Mathematical Practices — Problem Solving and Reasoning
coherence_reason: The cluster includes both mathematical practices and unrelated reading comprehension skills; two main sub-concepts are present.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can tell if a simple pattern made with objects or symbols is the same or different.
Your child can find two objects that have something in common, like color or size, when given a group of objects.
Your child can think about shapes and their features.
Your child can sort and organize up to 10 objects by one feature, like color or shape, using pictures or real items.
Your child knows who the leaders are in your town and state and what they do.
Your child uses tools to explore the features of triangles and circles.
Your child understands how Alabama’s five geographic regions affected where settlers moved in the early 1800s.
Your child can observe and describe shapes by their important features, sort and draw them, and begin learning about fractions by dividing shapes.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Geometry — Shape Attributes and Classification
coherence_reason: Most standards pertain to shapes, their attributes, and classification, but two standards are unrelated (about civic leaders and geographic regions).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that shapes like rhombuses, rectangles, and squares share some features, and can name bigger groups they belong to, like quadrilaterals.
Your child can talk about the features of flat shapes, like how many sides or corners they have.
Your child can name and point out a circle, square, and triangle.
Your child can describe how many sides, corners, or angles a flat shape has.
Your child can find a side or corner (angle) on common flat shapes.
Your child uses tools to identify, describe, and draw special four-sided shapes and sorts flat shapes based on their features.
Your child can sort and describe flat shapes and solid figures like cones, cylinders, spheres, and different prisms using correct geometry words.
Your child can recognize and draw rhombuses, parallelograms, trapezoids, rectangles, and squares, and also draw four-sided shapes that don’t fit these types.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Geometry — Properties and Classification of Shapes
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on identifying, describing, and classifying flat shapes (polygons, especially quadrilaterals), but one also mentions solid figures and another mentions circles, indicating minor variation in emphasis.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use a ruler to measure how long something is to the nearest whole number.
Your child can tell how long something is by using everyday items to measure it.
Your child can measure lengths using rulers with halves and quarters of an inch and show the results on a line plot.
Your child can tell which object is longer or shorter when the difference is obvious.
Your child can measure the length of objects using standard tools like rulers or yardsticks.
Your child can measure lengths using rulers with halves and quarters of an inch and make line plots to show the measurements.
Your child can choose and use the right tools to measure how long something is.
Your child can measure lengths using rulers marked in halves and quarters of an inch and make a line plot to show the measurements.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Measurement — Using Tools and Representing Lengths
coherence_reason: All standards focus on measuring lengths using standard tools and representing those measurements, sometimes with line plots.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can compare different types of economies (like traditional, command, market, and mixed), and explain what makes each one good or bad.
Your child can draw picture and bar graphs with correct scales to show data and solve simple problems about how many more or less.
Your child can answer questions about information shown in a simple bar graph or pictograph.
Represent and interpret data.
Your child can look at a picture or bar graph and answer questions about the data.
Your child can solve one- and two-step problems using data shown in tables, dot plots, picture graphs, or bar graphs.
Your child can summarize information with many categories using tables, dot plots, picture graphs, or bar graphs.
Your child can make daily schedules, calendars, and timelines to organize events.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Data — Representing and Interpreting Data with Graphs
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on interpreting and representing data with graphs, but one standard is about economies and another about organizing events with timelines, which are only loosely related.
split_topics: Representing and Interpreting Data with Graphs

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child works well with others in group activities.
Attend to precision.
Your child understands how to measure things, collect data, and learn about chance.
Your child shows interest and stays focused while learning multiplication.
Your child shows interest and participates actively when learning about sorting and graphing data.
Your child shows interest and participates actively when learning about fractions.
Your child shows interest and participates actively when learning about area.
Your child shows interest and focus while learning about numbers and math.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Mathematical Practices — Engagement and Participation
coherence_reason: The standards group together general math engagement, social skills, mathematical practices, and several specific content areas, which are distinct topics.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100 using place value.
Your child can place a number on a number line between two multiples of 10, 100, 1,000, or 10,000 and use words to explain how close the number is when rounding.
Your child can round numbers to the nearest ten or hundred and use easy numbers to estimate answers for addition and subtraction problems.
Your child can round numbers up to 1,000 to the nearest 100 using pictures or models.
Your child can round whole numbers from 0 to 1,000 to the nearest 10 or 100.
Your child can check if answers make sense by using mental math and estimation like rounding.
Your child can round two-digit numbers to the nearest ten and three-digit numbers to the nearest hundred.
Your child can round a number to the nearest ten using number lines or grids, like rounding 22 to 20.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Place Value — Rounding Whole Numbers
coherence_reason: All standards focus on rounding whole numbers using place value concepts, including using number lines, models, and estimation strategies.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands multiplication as finding the total number in several equal groups.
Your child can use repeated addition to find out how many objects there are in total.
Your child understands that a multiplication expression, like 3 x 24, means 3 times 24 or comparing groups of the same size.
Collaboration
Your child understands multiplication by showing it as repeated addition, groups, arrays, jumps on a number line, or skip counting.
Your child can show multiplication tables using repeated addition, equal groups, arrays, area models, equal jumps on a number line, or skip counting.
Your child understands multiplication as adding equal groups and can explain what the total means.
Your child can use the associative rule for multiplication to group numbers differently when multiplying.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Multiplication — Equal Groups, Repeated Addition, and Representations
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on understanding and representing multiplication through equal groups and repeated addition using multiple models, but one standard about the associative rule introduces a minor sub-concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands division as sharing objects equally or grouping them into equal parts.
Your child can tell which shapes are divided into equal parts, like halves or fourths, by looking at them.
Your child can recognize when a shape is split into two equal parts (one-half).
Your child can tell the difference between a whole object and just part of an object.
Your child can tell the difference between a part of something (like half a cookie) and the whole thing.
Your child can use objects or pictures to show a whole item or half of an item.
Your child can divide shapes into equal parts with the same area and say the area of each part as a fraction of the whole.
Your child understands that shapes can be divided into equal areas.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Fractions — Equal Parts and Area of Shapes
coherence_reason: The standards reference both the concept of dividing shapes into equal parts (fractions/geometry) and the concept of division as sharing/grouping, which are related but distinct sub-concepts.
split_topics: Equal Parts and Fractions in Shapes

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that 1/b means one part when a whole is split into b equal parts, and a/b means a parts of size 1/b.
Your child can break apart and put together a fraction a/b (where a is more than zero and less than or equal to b) as a sum of parts 1/b.
Your child understands that the fraction 1/b means one equal part when a whole is divided into b equal parts, and b is a number not equal to zero.
Your child understands the formal way to define a derivative at a point and what it means for a function to be differentiable.
Your child understands that a fraction like 1/4 means one part when a whole is split into four equal parts, and a fraction like 2/4 means two of those parts.
Your child understands fractions as adding the same small fraction multiple times.
Your child can understand fractions as adding the same small part (like 1/3) several times to make a bigger part.
Your child understands a unit fraction as one part when a whole is split into equal parts.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Fractions — Unit Fractions and Fraction Sums
coherence_reason: All but one standard focus on understanding unit fractions and composing fractions from unit fractions; one unrelated standard about derivatives is present.
split_topics: Composing fractions as sums of unit fractions

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands how numbers are made up of tens and ones.
Your child understands how numbers work in the base-10 system up to the hundred-thousands place.
Your child can break apart and build numbers up to 100,000 by adding ten-thousands, thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones using objects, pictures, and numbers.
Your child can compare and put in order whole numbers up to 100,000 and use >, <, or = to show which numbers are bigger or smaller.
Your child can break apart and put together four-digit numbers in different ways using thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones, showing this with objects, drawings, or equations.
Your child can tell if a number from 1 to 100 is even or odd.
Your child can break apart and put together three-digit numbers using hundreds, tens, and ones with objects or drawings.
Your child can read and write numbers from 0 to 10,000 in regular numbers, expanded form, and words.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Place Value — Understanding and Composing Numbers
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on place value concepts and composing/decomposing numbers, but the inclusion of even/odd number identification introduces a minor variation.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can write whole numbers as fractions and recognize when fractions are equal to whole numbers.
Your child can show whole numbers as fractions and find fractions that are equal to whole numbers.
Your child can read and write fractions up to one whole using standard fraction form.
Your child can read and write fractions, including those greater than one, using numbers and words.
Your child can write whole numbers as fractions and find fractions that equal whole numbers.
Your child can write whole numbers as fractions and find fractions that are equal to whole numbers.
Your child can show, name, and write fractions or mixed numbers with denominators like 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, or 10 using different models.
Your child can count parts of a fraction bigger than one and write it as an improper fraction and a mixed number.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Fractions — Whole Numbers as Fractions and Mixed Numbers
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on expressing whole numbers as fractions and identifying fractions equal to whole numbers, but a few reference improper fractions and mixed numbers, slightly broadening the concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can compare two fractions with the same top or bottom number by thinking about their size, and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.
Your child can tell which group has more or less when looking at two groups with a big difference in size.
Your child can compare two fractions that have the same top or bottom number and explain which is bigger using pictures, objects, or words.
Your child can compare two fractions with the same top or bottom number by thinking about their sizes and explaining the answer using symbols, words, objects, or pictures.
Your child can compare two fractions that have the same top number or the same bottom number by thinking about their size, knowing the fractions must be parts of the same whole.
Your child can plot, order, and compare fractions that have the same top number or the same bottom number.
Your child can compare two fractions with the same top number or same bottom number by thinking about their size, use >, =, or < to show the comparison, and explain why.
Your child can compare two fractions with the same bottom number using >, =, or < and explain why.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Fractions — Comparing Fractions with Like Numerators or Denominators
coherence_reason: All standards focus on comparing two fractions with either the same numerator or denominator, using reasoning, symbols, and explanations.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use a picture or model to show one-half or one-fourth of something.
Your child can find and explain simple fractions that are equal, like 1/2 and 2/4, using pictures or models.
Your child understands that two fractions are equal if they represent the same amount or point on a number line.
Your child can explain when fractions are equal and compare their sizes by thinking about what they mean.
Your child can solve problems where they divide things into parts or share them among people using pictures of fractions with denominators like 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8.
Your child can show fractions between zero and one with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, or 8 using objects, pictures, strip diagrams, and number lines.
Your child can show equal fractions with denominators like 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 using objects, pictures, or number lines.
Your child understands that two fractions are equal if they show the same point on a number line or cover the same part of a whole shape.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Fractions — Representing and Identifying Equivalent Fractions
coherence_reason: All standards focus on representing, identifying, and explaining equivalent fractions using models, pictures, and number lines with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands fractions as numbers on a number line and can show them there.
Your child is learning to understand fractions as numbers.
Your child can find the fraction between zero and one with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, or 8 that matches a specific point on a number line.
Your child can show halves, fourths, and eighths as distances on a number line starting at zero.
Your child understands fractions as numbers on a number line and can show fractions on a number line.
Your child can show a fraction like 1/4 on a number line by dividing the line from 0 to 1 into equal parts and find where 1/4 is located.
Your child learns that fractions are numbers and how they work.
Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross to find and name points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Fractions — Fractions as Numbers on the Number Line
coherence_reason: All but one standard focus on understanding and representing fractions as numbers on the number line; one standard introduces coordinate pairs, which is a related but distinct subtopic.
split_topics: Fractions as numbers on the number line
Using number lines to identify coordinates

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can show a fraction a/b on a number line by marking off a parts of size 1/b starting from zero.
Your child can show the fraction 1/b on a number line by dividing the space from 0 to 1 into b equal parts.
Your child can show the fraction 1/b on a number line by dividing the space from 0 to 1 into b equal parts and locating 1/b.
Your child can show the fraction 1/b on a number line by dividing the space from 0 to 1 into equal parts and identifying one part.
Your child can show a fraction like a/b on a number line by marking off equal parts and locating the fraction’s position, even if it’s more than 1.
Your child can show one part of a whole on a number line by dividing the space between 0 and 1 into equal parts.
Your child can show a fraction like a/b on a number line by marking off equal parts of size 1/b from zero and seeing where a/b lands.
Your child can show a fraction 1/b on a number line by dividing the space from 0 to 1 into b equal parts and identifying one part as 1/b.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Fractions — Representing Fractions on the Number Line
coherence_reason: All standards focus on representing fractions, specifically 1/b and a/b, on the number line by partitioning into equal parts and locating the fraction’s position.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can multiply one-digit numbers by multiples of 10 like 20 or 90 using place value ideas.
Your child can multiply a two-digit number by a one-digit number using different methods like mental math, breaking numbers apart, or the usual step-by-step way.
Your child can multiply a two-digit number by a one-digit number using different methods like mental math, partial products, and properties of multiplication.
Your child can multiply one-digit numbers by multiples of 10 (like 9 × 80) using place value and math rules.
Your child can use the distributive property to multiply a one-digit number by a two-digit number and use multiplication properties to multiply one-digit numbers.
Your child can use objects to learn how to multiply two one-digit numbers.
Your child can multiply a one-digit number by multiples of 10 (up to 90) or 100 (up to 900) accurately.
Your child uses the idea that numbers can be multiplied in any order to find answers with one-digit numbers.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Multiplication — One-Digit Multipliers, Multiples of 10, and Two-Digit Numbers
coherence_reason: All standards focus on multiplication involving one-digit multipliers, multiples of 10, and two-digit numbers, with minor variation in emphasis such as concrete models and properties.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands division as finding a missing number in multiplication.
Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division equations with three numbers.
Your child can find the missing whole number in multiplication or division problems involving three numbers, whether the missing number is a factor or the product.
Your child understands division as finding the missing number in a multiplication problem.
Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division equations with three whole numbers.
Your child explores how division is related to multiplication by finding missing numbers.
Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division problems with small numbers.
Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division problems with three whole numbers, no matter where the missing number is.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Multiplication and Division — Finding the Missing Number
coherence_reason: All standards focus on students finding the missing number in multiplication and division equations with three numbers, with only minor wording differences.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve addition and subtraction problems where the answer is up to 20.
Your child can solve real-life problems in one step by adding or subtracting numbers up to 20.
Your child uses place value and math rules to add, subtract, multiply, and divide bigger numbers.
Your child can add and subtract numbers with answers up to 10.
Your child can solve one-step real-life problems by adding or subtracting numbers up to 20.
Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 quickly using place value and other strategies.
Your child can solve addition and subtraction problems with answers up to 20.
Your child can solve one-step real-life problems by adding or subtracting numbers up to 10.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Addition and Subtraction — Sums and Differences within 20
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on addition and subtraction within 20, but some mention sums within 10 and others reference using place value strategies for numbers up to 1000, indicating two related but distinct sub-concepts.
split_topics: Addition and subtraction of larger numbers using place value

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can multiply and divide numbers up to 100.
Your child can multiply and divide numbers up to 100 quickly and knows all multiplication facts for one-digit numbers by memory.
Your child can quickly recall multiplication facts up to 10 by 10 and knows the related division facts.
Your child can solve one- and two-step multiplication and division problems up to 100 using arrays, strip diagrams, and equations.
Your child can solve one-step and two-step multiplication and division problems within 100 using objects, pictures, or math facts.
Your child will quickly remember multiplication facts up to 10 times 10 and also know the related division facts.
Your child can solve one- and two-step multiplication and division problems up to 100 using objects, pictures, arrays, area models, equal groups, operation properties, or multiplication facts.
Your child can quickly multiply and divide numbers up to 100 and knows all multiplication facts for one-digit numbers by memory.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Multiplication and Division — Facts and Problem Solving within 100
coherence_reason: All standards focus on multiplication and division facts and problem solving with products and dividends up to 100, including quick recall and related division facts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find and recognize patterns in numbers, like skip counting or repeated addition.
Your child can make, describe, and continue simple number patterns.
Your child can make, talk about, and continue simple patterns using numbers, objects, or symbols.
Your child can solve problems using all four operations and recognize patterns in math.
Your child can find and explain number patterns in addition and multiplication tables.
Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.
Your child can tell if a number is even or odd by using simple rules about divisibility.
Your child can show everyday relationships using pairs of numbers in tables and explain them in words.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Patterns — Number Patterns and Relationships
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on identifying, describing, and continuing number patterns, with minor inclusion of related ideas such as even/odd and relationships in tables.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can pick the right tool, like a scale or ruler, to measure something when needed.
Your child can choose the right tool to solve simple word problems about weight or liquid amounts.
Your child can measure and estimate liquid amounts and weights using grams, kilograms, and liters, and solve simple math problems about them.
Your child can identify tools like scales for weight and measuring cups for liquids.
Your child can solve problems about measuring and estimating time, liquids, and weights.
Your child can name tools used to measure weight (scale) and liquid (measuring cups).
Your child can find the amount of liquid or weight using the right tools and units.
Your child can decide when to measure liquid volume or weight depending on the situation.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Measurement — Tools, Units, and Problem Solving with Mass and Volume
coherence_reason: All standards focus on selecting and using appropriate measurement tools and units for mass and volume, and applying these skills to solve problems.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division and check if answers make sense.
Your child can solve and make two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division and check if answers make sense by estimating.
Your child can solve one- and two-step real-life problems using addition and subtraction with measurements like length, temperature, or liquid volume.
Your child can solve real-life problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division with whole-number lengths, weights, temperatures, or liquid amounts.
Your child can solve one- and two-step real-life problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division with whole numbers.
Your child can solve two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.
Your child can add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve simple word problems about mass or liquid volume when the units are the same.
Your child can write equations with letters to represent unknown numbers in problems.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Problem Solving — Two-Step Word Problems with the Four Operations
coherence_reason: All standards focus on solving one- and two-step problems with the four operations, but a few emphasize measurement contexts or the use of equations with variables, introducing minor variations.
split_topics: Two-step word problems with four operations

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child uses math rules to help multiply and divide.
Your child can solve word problems using multiplication and division with equal groups, arrays, or measurements, using drawings or equations.
Your child can represent and solve problems using multiplication and division.
Your child understands multiplication rules and how multiplication and division are related.
Your child can find the answer to division problems by using what they know about multiplication.
Your child can find the answer to division problems by using the connection between multiplication and division.
Your child can explain and use math rules to help multiply and divide, like switching numbers around or breaking problems into parts.
Your child understands and uses the chain rule to find derivatives of combined functions.
canonical_name: Grade 3 Math: Multiplication and Division — Relationships and Problem Solving
coherence_reason: All standards except one focus on multiplication/division relationships and problem-solving; one standard about derivatives (chain rule) is unrelated but appears to be an error.
split_topics: Multiplication and division relationships and problem solving
